title: DNS A/AAAA Records
description: Async check: resolves A and AAAA records for the target hostname and flags private/loopback addresses that should never appear in DNS for a public target.
evidence: Executed via dns/promises.resolve4 and resolve6.
riskImpact: Private IPs in public DNS can leak internal infrastructure and indicate takeover risk for dangling records.
explanation: DNS resolution is the first step of every scan. If the resolver returns RFC1918 / loopback / link-local addresses for a hostname the user typed, the target isn't publicly reachable and the scan is blocked.
fixSteps: Remove A/AAAA records pointing at internal IPs.
Audit CNAMEs for dangling-takeover risk (cloudfront, heroku, etc.).
Use split-horizon DNS only where appropriate.

codeExamples.label: dig
codeExamples.code: dig +short example.com A	dig +short example.com AAAA

title: CAA Record Missing
description: CAA records constrain which CAs can issue certificates for your domain.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-caa-record-missing.
riskImpact: Publish a CAA record: 0 issue 'letsencrypt.org'
explanation: CAA records constrain which CAs can issue certificates for your domain.
fixSteps: Publish a CAA record: 0 issue 'letsencrypt.org'

title: Less than 2 authoritative nameservers
description: RFC 1035 requires at least two authoritative nameservers for redundancy.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-ns-record-count.
riskImpact: Add a second NS provider (or two SOA records at different subnets)
explanation: RFC 1035 requires at least two authoritative nameservers for redundancy.
fixSteps: Add a second NS provider (or two SOA records at different subnets)

title: MX Record Missing
description: Domains that send mail should have an MX record; missing MX means backscatter or rejected mail.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-mx-record-missing.
riskImpact: Add an MX record if you send mail from this domain
explanation: Domains that send mail should have an MX record; missing MX means backscatter or rejected mail.
fixSteps: Add an MX record if you send mail from this domain

title: No backup MX (priority > 20)
description: Backup MX servers (priority > 20) catch mail when the primary is down.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-mx-backup-record.
riskImpact: Add a backup MX with priority 20 or 30
explanation: Backup MX servers (priority > 20) catch mail when the primary is down.
fixSteps: Add a backup MX with priority 20 or 30

title: No SRV records for common services
description: SRV records publish service endpoints (_autodiscover._tcp, _sip._udp, etc.).
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-srv-records-missing.
riskImpact: If you use Exchange / SIP / XMPP, publish the SRV records
explanation: SRV records publish service endpoints (_autodiscover._tcp, _sip._udp, etc.).
fixSteps: If you use Exchange / SIP / XMPP, publish the SRV records

title: SOA refresh > 24h
description: SOA refresh > 24h slows propagation when you change NS records.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-soa-refresh-high.
riskImpact: Lower refresh to 3600-7200 seconds for faster rollovers
explanation: SOA refresh > 24h slows propagation when you change NS records.
fixSteps: Lower refresh to 3600-7200 seconds for faster rollovers

title: TLSA (DANE) Record Missing
description: TLSA records pin the certificate for TLS-over-DNS. Optional but defense-in-depth.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-tlsa-record-missing.
riskImpact: Publish a TLSA record at _443._tcp.yourdomain.com if you want DANE
explanation: TLSA records pin the certificate for TLS-over-DNS. Optional but defense-in-depth.
fixSteps: Publish a TLSA record at _443._tcp.yourdomain.com if you want DANE

title: Authoritative DNS exposed on public IPs
description: Authoritative nameservers reachable on the public internet can be queried directly, leaking your zone data.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-open-dns-resolver.
riskImpact: Restrict AXFR to internal IPs only
explanation: Authoritative nameservers reachable on the public internet can be queried directly, leaking your zone data.
fixSteps: Restrict AXFR to internal IPs only

title: Dangling CNAME Record (subdomain takeover risk)
description: A CNAME pointing to a defunct service (heroku, aws, fastly) lets an attacker register the orphan and serve content on your subdomain.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-dangling-cname.
riskImpact: Audit CNAMEs against the cloud providers you use; remove dangling entries
explanation: A CNAME pointing to a defunct service (heroku, aws, fastly) lets an attacker register the orphan and serve content on your subdomain.
fixSteps: Audit CNAMEs against the cloud providers you use; remove dangling entries

title: DNS Zone Transfer (AXFR) Allowed
description: AXFR responses leak your entire zone. Restrict to authorized secondaries only.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-zone-transfer-allowed.
riskImpact: Set allow-transfer { <slave-ips>; }; in BIND, or equivalent in your DNS provider
explanation: AXFR responses leak your entire zone. Restrict to authorized secondaries only.
fixSteps: Set allow-transfer { <slave-ips>; }; in BIND, or equivalent in your DNS provider

title: Authoritative DNS Allows Recursion
description: Authoritative nameservers shouldn't also be recursive resolvers (open resolver risk).
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-recursion-enabled.
riskImpact: Set allow-recursion { none; }; or split authoritative and recursive roles
explanation: Authoritative nameservers shouldn't also be recursive resolvers (open resolver risk).
fixSteps: Set allow-recursion { none; }; or split authoritative and recursive roles

title: NXDOMAIN Hijack Risk (ISP NXDOMAIN Replacement)
description: Some ISPs replace NXDOMAIN responses with ads. DNSSEC + a validating resolver prevents this.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-nxdomain-hijack-risk.
riskImpact: Enable DNSSEC and use a validating resolver (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8)
explanation: Some ISPs replace NXDOMAIN responses with ads. DNSSEC + a validating resolver prevents this.
fixSteps: Enable DNSSEC and use a validating resolver (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8)

title: NAPTR Records Present
description: NAPTR (Naming Authority Pointer) records enable ENUM, SIP, and service discovery. Their presence reveals telephony or federation endpoints.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-naptr-record-present.
riskImpact: Discovered NAPTR targets can be probed by attackers for unauthorized federation or toll-fraud attempts.
explanation: NAPTR (Naming Authority Pointer) records enable ENUM, SIP, and service discovery. Their presence reveals telephony or federation endpoints.
fixSteps: Confirm NAPTR records are intentional and only point at approved carriers.
Restrict SIP/ENUM exposure with allowlists on your SBC.

title: LOC Records Disclose Geographic Location
description: LOC records publish the geographic coordinates of a host. Public LOC records disclose physical location to anyone querying DNS.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-loc-record-present.
riskImpact: Attackers can use disclosed coordinates to map your infrastructure geography and plan physical or social-engineering attacks.
explanation: LOC records publish the geographic coordinates of a host. Public LOC records disclose physical location to anyone querying DNS.
fixSteps: Remove LOC records unless required by a public service.
If kept, round coordinates to nearest 0.1 degree to reduce precision.

title: SSHFP Records Missing
description: SSHFP records publish SSH host-key fingerprints in DNS, enabling DNSSEC-validated TOFU and preventing MITM on SSH.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-sshfp-record-missing.
riskImpact: Without SSHFP + DNSSEC, SSH clients cannot cryptographically verify host identity on first connect.
explanation: SSHFP records publish SSH host-key fingerprints in DNS, enabling DNSSEC-validated TOFU and preventing MITM on SSH.
fixSteps: Generate SSHFP records with ssh-keygen -r <hostname>.
Publish them under <hostname> as SSHFP type 2 (SHA-256).
Enable DNSSEC and use VerifyHostKeyDNS yes in ssh_config.

title: DNSSEC Delegation Signer (DS) Record Missing
description: DS records in the parent zone prove the chain of trust for DNSSEC. Missing DS means your signed zone is not validated by resolvers.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-ds-record-missing.
riskImpact: Without DS, resolvers treat your domain as DNSSEC-insecure, defeating the spoofing protection DNSSEC provides.
explanation: DS records in the parent zone prove the chain of trust for DNSSEC. Missing DS means your signed zone is not validated by resolvers.
fixSteps: Publish a DS record at the parent zone pointing at your DNSKEY.
Confirm with dig +short DS <your domain>.

title: DNSSEC DNSKEY Record Missing
description: DNSKEY publishes the public key used to verify RRSIGs. Without it, no resolver can validate signatures for the zone.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-dnskey-record-missing.
riskImpact: Missing DNSKEY means DNSSEC validation cannot proceed past your zone, breaking trust-chain for subdomains.
explanation: DNSKEY publishes the public key used to verify RRSIGs. Without it, no resolver can validate signatures for the zone.
fixSteps: Enable DNSSEC at your DNS provider and publish both KSK and ZSK.
Roll keys per your operator policy (typically annually for KSK).

title: DNSSEC RRSIG Record Missing
description: RRSIG records carry the cryptographic signature over a RRset. Without RRSIGs, validating resolvers reject your responses as bogus.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-rrsig-record-missing.
riskImpact: Missing RRSIG causes SERVFAIL for DNSSEC-validating resolvers, breaking reachability for security-conscious users.
explanation: RRSIG records carry the cryptographic signature over a RRset. Without RRSIGs, validating resolvers reject your responses as bogus.
fixSteps: Re-sign the zone with your DNSSEC-aware authoritative server (BIND, Knot, etc.).
Verify with dig +dnssec <your domain> and check the ad flag in the response.

title: NSEC/NSEC3 Zone Walking Enabled
description: NSEC records form a linked list of existing names that can be walked to enumerate every name in the zone. NSEC3 with opt-out partially mitigates but still leaks hashed names.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-nsec-zone-walking.
riskImpact: Attackers can enumerate internal hostnames (admin, staging, dev, vpn) you did not intend to publish.
explanation: NSEC records form a linked list of existing names that can be walked to enumerate every name in the zone. NSEC3 with opt-out partially mitigates but still leaks hashed names.
fixSteps: Use NSEC3 with opt-out and a strong, randomly rotated salt.
Consider NSEC5 or aggressive cache-minimum if your server supports it.
Avoid NSEC for zones that must hide internal names.

title: Dangling CNAME (CDN/PaaS Takeover)
description: CNAMEs pointing at GitHub Pages (github.io), Heroku (herokuapp.com), AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN (azurewebsites.net/azureedge.net), Fastly, or Netlify that no longer resolve let attackers register the orphan and serve content from your subdomain.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-dangling-cname-cdn-paas.
riskImpact: Subdomain takeover via these providers enables phishing, cookie theft, and bypass of CSP/origin controls.
explanation: CNAMEs pointing at GitHub Pages (github.io), Heroku (herokuapp.com), AWS CloudFront, Azure CDN (azurewebsites.net/azureedge.net), Fastly, or Netlify that no longer resolve let attackers register the orphan and serve content from your subdomain.
fixSteps: Inventory all CNAMEs targeting GitHub, Heroku, AWS, Azure, Fastly, and Netlify.
Remove CNAMEs whose backing service no longer exists.
Use can-i-take-over-xyz to confirm takeover viability per provider.

title: Dangling CNAME (SaaS Takeover)
description: CNAMEs pointing at Zendesk, HelpScout, Intercom, Drift, statuspage.io, pingdom, Pantheon, Acquia, or WP Engine that no longer resolve let attackers claim the namespace on your subdomain.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-dangling-cname-saas.
riskImpact: SaaS subdomain takeover enables phishing and impersonation of support/help-center pages, eroding customer trust.
explanation: CNAMEs pointing at Zendesk, HelpScout, Intercom, Drift, statuspage.io, pingdom, Pantheon, Acquia, or WP Engine that no longer resolve let attackers claim the namespace on your subdomain.
fixSteps: Audit all CNAMEs targeting customer-support SaaS providers.
Remove dangling entries; if the integration is still required, re-register and re-point.
Monitor for stale CNAMEs with automated tooling (subjack, nuclei, can-i-take-over-xyz).

title: DNS-over-HTTPS Provider Detected
description: DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) endpoints reachable from your origin (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8, 9.9.9.9, dns.quad9.net) indicate upstream resolvers used by your stack and confirm DoH connectivity.
evidence: Async / body / header check for dns-doh-provider-detected.
riskImpact: Public DoH providers in your DNS path leak query patterns to third parties; document them in your data-flow diagram.
explanation: DNS-over-HTTPS (DoH) endpoints reachable from your origin (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8, 9.9.9.9, dns.quad9.net) indicate upstream resolvers used by your stack and confirm DoH connectivity.
fixSteps: Document which DoH provider your resolvers use in the security policy.
For sensitive workloads, run a validating recursive resolver (Unbound, Knot Resolver) and only allow egress to your chosen upstream.
